1: 'Oh that my head were a spring of water and my eyes a fountain of tears, so that I could weep day and night for the slain of my people!' Jeremiah's tears are a prophetic type of Christ's tears. Jesus weeps over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41: eklausen ep' autēn, he wept over it) with the same combination of prophetic grief and divine anguish. John 11:35 ('Jesus wept') — the shortest verse in the NT — shows the same tear-fountain at the death of Lazarus. The weeping prophet is fulfilled in the weeping Son: YHWH's grief for his people becomes incarnate grief.
2: 'Oh that I had a lodging place in the wilderness, so that I could leave my people and go away — for they are all adulterers, a crowd of treacherous people!' The prophet's exhausted longing for escape from a faithless people. Jesus never expresses this longing — he goes through the temptation to withdraw (Gethsemane) but chooses to stay and drink the cup. Where Jeremiah longed to leave, Christ chose to remain: 'I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you' (John 14:18). His determination to remain with his people fulfills what the prophet yearned to abandon.
3: The tongue as a bow shooting falsehood (yidreku et-leshonam qeshtam sheqer). The community of deceit — powerful in the land but not by truth. James 3:2-12 elaborates the tongue-as-weapon image: the tongue is 'a fire, a world of evil among the parts of the body.' Jesus addresses the tongue-and-truth issue directly: 'let your yes be yes and your no be no; anything more comes from the evil one' (Matthew 5:37). He himself is the Truth (John 14:6) — the counterpart of the land of deceit.
4: 'Every brother is a schemer' (kol-ach aqov ya-aqov — playing on 'Jacob', the heel-grabber/schemer). The universal distrust within the covenant community. Jesus enters this community of treachery and is himself betrayed by a close companion (John 13:18; Psalms 41:9 — 'even my close friend who shared my bread has lifted his heel against me'). He knows the Jacob-pattern from inside human experience — and his self-giving into betrayal reverses the pattern: the one who could have deceived chose radical transparency.
5: Universal deception, tongues trained to lie, exhausted by iniquity. Jesus's testimony: 'I have spoken openly to the world; I always taught in synagogues or at the temple... I said nothing in secret' (John 18:20). He is the anti-type of the deceptive tongue: total transparency, full disclosure, the word that cannot be falsified. In him the community of trained liars encounters the one who cannot lie (Titus 1:2: 'God, who does not lie').
6: 'You live in the midst of deception; through their deceit they refuse to know me, declares the LORD.' The knowledge-of-God failure at the root of all the deception. Jesus is the definitive revelation of the Father: 'Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father' (John 14:9); 'No one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him' (Matthew 11:27). Refusing to know Jesus is refusing to know the LORD — the same refusal Jeremiah identifies as the community's core failure.
7: 'I will refine them and test them — for what else can I do with my people?' The divine refining through judgment — the only remaining option when all other means of restoration have failed. Jesus himself is the refiner: 'he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire' (Matthew 3:11-12); 'his winnowing fork is in his hand' (Matthew 3:12). The refinement through the cross — bearing the judgment fire himself — is the ultimate form of YHWH's refining of his people.
8: The tongue as lethal arrow, speaking peace with the mouth while ambushing in the heart. Judas's betrayal kiss (Matthew 26:49: 'Greetings, Rabbi!' — peace-speech with a treacherous purpose) is the exact fulfillment of this pattern. The one who kisses Jesus to betray him enacts the 'peace in the mouth, ambush in the heart' dynamic of Jeremiah 9:8. Jesus receives the ambush in the mouth and bears it without retaliating in kind.
9: 'Should I not punish them? Should I not take vengeance on such a nation?' The divine justice that demands accountability. Jesus takes the full weight of this divine vengeance in his own person on the cross: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The vengeance YHWH takes on the faithless nation is absorbed by the faithful Son — so that the nation does not bear it, but lives through his bearing.
10: Weeping and wailing for the mountains, lament for the wilderness pastures: the land is desolate because everyone has left. Jesus's lament over the cities of Galilee (Matthew 11:20-24: 'woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida!') uses the same lament-over-land pattern: the city that rejected his ministry will be more desolate than Sodom and Gomorrah. The land suffers when YHWH's word is rejected.
11: Jerusalem a heap of rubble, a haunt of jackals; cities of Judah desolate. Fulfilled in 70 CE — the destruction of Jerusalem that Jesus announced (Matthew 24:2; Luke 21:20-24: 'let those in Judea flee to the mountains...'). The jackals-in-Jerusalem image that Jeremiah saw in vision, Jesus sees approaching and announces as imminent. The gap between Jeremiah's oracle and the destruction of Jerusalem mirrors the gap between Jesus's oracle and 70 CE.
12: 'Who is wise enough to understand this? Who has received the word from the LORD and can explain it?' The call for an interpreter of disaster. Jesus is the ultimate interpreter of the divine purpose in judgment: he explains to the disciples on the Emmaus road 'everything in the Scriptures concerning himself' (Luke 24:27), including why it was necessary for the Messiah to suffer. He has received the word from the LORD — 'my teaching is not my own; it comes from the one who sent me' (John 7:16).
13: The LORD's explanation: they abandoned my law (torati) that I placed before them, did not obey, and did not walk in it. Jesus frames his own teaching as the internalized Torah: 'I will put my laws in their minds and write them on their hearts' (Hebrews 8:10 quoting Jeremiah 31:33). The external Torah abandoned by Jeremiah's generation becomes the internal Torah inscribed by the Spirit — the new covenant solution to the old covenant failure.
14: They followed the stubbornness of their hearts and went after the Baals as their ancestors taught them. The intergenerational transmission of idolatry — taught apostasy rather than taught Torah (cf. Deuteronomy 6:7). Jesus teaches his disciples to make new disciples and teach them (Matthew 28:19-20) — the intergenerational transmission of covenant faithfulness that reverses the intergenerational transmission of Baal worship.
15: Wormwood and poisoned water fed to the people because of apostasy. The bitter waters of judgment. Revelation 8:10-11 uses the wormwood image for the end-time judgment: 'a great star ... fell on a third of the rivers ... the name of the star is Wormwood.' Jesus, at the cross, is offered wine mixed with gall (Matthew 27:34) — the bitter draught, which he initially refuses — then drinks the full cup of suffering (Jeremiah 9:42: 'let him come down from the cross'). He drinks the wormwood so the covenant people do not have to drink it permanently.
16: Scattered among nations, pursued by the sword until consumed. The comprehensive exile. Jesus's own flight to Egypt as an infant (Matthew 2:13-15) makes him a participant in the diaspora pattern — 'out of Egypt I called my son' (Hosea 11:1, quoted in Matthew 2:15). He is the true Israel in exile who returns; his resurrection is the return from the ultimate 'exile' of death.
17: Summon the mourning women — the professional lament-singers. The organized mourning liturgy of the ancient Near East: trained women who led communal grief. Jesus encounters such mourning in the NT: at Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:38-39: 'people crying and wailing loudly') and at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:31-33). In both cases he transforms mourning into resurrection hope: 'she is not dead but sleeping' (Mark 5:39); 'your brother will rise again' (John 11:23). The mourning-women tradition is not abolished but superseded.
18: Tears streaming from eyes, eyelids overflowing with water — the lament that YHWH calls for. Jesus in Gethsemane: 'being in anguish, he prayed more earnestly, and his sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground' (Luke 22:44) — the physical intensity of his grief prefigures the weeping and tears that Hebrews 5:7 attributes to him: 'during the days of Jesus's life on earth, he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears.' The weeping of 9:18 has its NT fulfillment in the Son's tears.
19: 'We are devastated! We are utterly disgraced — because we have had to leave the land and our houses have been torn down.' The exile's lament — loss of land and dwelling as the expression of covenant loss. Jesus promises a dwelling that no exile can take away: 'in my Father's house are many rooms ... I am going to prepare a place for you' (John 14:2). The permanence of the dwelling he prepares reverses the impermanence of the houses torn down in exile.
20: Women commanded to hear the word of the LORD — teach your daughters to mourn; teach one another the lament. The female transmission of grief. The women at the cross (Luke 23:27-31) enact this: Jesus addresses them directly: 'daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me; weep for yourselves and for your children.' He directs their grief toward the coming destruction of 70 CE — the lament-teaching that Jeremiah prescribes is here applied by Jesus to his own generation.
21: Death climbing through windows, entering palaces, cutting down children in the streets. The invasion of death into every space — no fortress sufficient. Jesus faces death in its full power and rises: 'Death has been swallowed up in victory. Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?' (1 Corinthians 15:54-55 citing Isaiah 25:8 and Hosea 13:14). Death's climb through the windows of Jeremiah 9:21 is reversed by Christ's victory over death in the resurrection.
22: 'Human corpses will fall like manure on the open field, like grain stalks left behind the reaper, with no one to gather them.' The exposed, ungathered dead of judgment. Jesus comes as the gatherer: 'the Son of Man will send out his angels and they will weed out of his kingdom everything that causes sin ... and gather his elect from the four winds' (Matthew 13:41; Jeremiah 24:31). The no-one-to-gather of 9:22 becomes the final gathering of the Son's harvest.
23: 'Let not the wise boast in their wisdom, nor the mighty in their strength, nor the rich in their riches.' The threefold prohibition of self-boasting. Paul cites this as the foundation of his entire theology of the cross: 'it is because of him that you are in Christ Jesus, who has become for us wisdom from God — that is, our righteousness, holiness and redemption. Therefore, as it is written: 'Let him who boasts boast in the Lord'' (1 Corinthians 1:30-31). The wisdom, strength, and riches that cannot be boasted are redefined and concentrated in Christ crucified.
24: 'But let him who boasts boast in this: that he understands and knows me — that I am the LORD who exercises steadfast love, justice, and righteousness on earth, for in these I delight.' To know YHWH as the one who does chesed, mishpat, and tzedaqah — and to boast only in that knowing. Jesus is the knowledge of God made accessible: 'anyone who has seen me has seen the Father' (John 14:9); 'now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent' (John 17:3). Boasting in knowing YHWH is now, in the NT, boasting in knowing Christ.
25: 'The days are coming when I will punish all who are only outwardly circumcised.' The critique of outward-only circumcision. Paul elaborates this in Romans 2:28-29: 'a person is not a Jew who is one outwardly, nor is circumcision merely outward and physical; no, a person is a Jew who is one inwardly, and circumcision is circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit.' Colossians 2:11-12 applies this to Christ: 'in him you were also circumcised with a circumcision not performed by human hands.' Christ provides the true circumcision of the heart that Jeremiah and Deuteronomy (Jeremiah 10:16; Jeremiah 30:6) required.
26: Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and the clipped-haired Arabs — all circumcised in flesh but uncircumcised in heart. The comprehensive list of the outwardly-circumcised but inwardly-uncircumcised dismantles any ethnic pride in covenant signs. Paul in Galatians and Romans makes the same argument globally: circumcision neither confers righteousness nor does its absence disqualify from covenant standing (Galatians 5:6: 'neither circumcision nor uncircumcision has any value; the only thing that counts is faith expressing itself through love'). The true Israel is defined by the heart-circumcision that Christ provides.
